lessons.title: Introduction to HTML
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what HTML is and what a tag does
I can use heading, paragraph and list tags to structure a page
I can write and view a simple HTML page in a browser
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: website · HTML · element · tag. TA: everyone has a code editor and a browser open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — how does a web page reach your screen? (think–pair–share with the browsers image). Pupils write/choose on the starter worksheet (minimal writing).
   • Support: tick the right answer.  • Core: explain why every browser opens the same file.  • Challenge: say what the server sends back.
2. (5–15) I-do — what is HTML? Tags in angle brackets; opening vs closing `/`. Map tags onto a newspaper (heading, subheading, paragraph) using the newspaper image.
3. (15–25) We-do (Predict). Pupils predict what a small page will show, then open and refresh it.
   • Support: match tag→job on the worksheet.  • Core: fill the “ul vs ol” answer.  • Challenge: explain why plain text is useful. Likely error: forgetting the closing `</tag>` → fix-words “every tag needs a partner that closes it with a /.”
4. (25–45) You-do — build a page with DOCTYPE/html/body, a heading, a paragraph and a list. Use the Parsons block to order a valid page first. Keep a screenshot.
   • Support: “type THIS line” card.  • Core: build unaided.  • Challenge: add an ordered list. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows their page in the browser. TA notes who closed every tag.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Images and links
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what accessibility means on a website
I can add an image to a page with the img tag
I can create a hyperlink with the a href tag
lessons.outline: Routine first. Vocabulary on board: image · hyperlink · accessibility · source (src). TA: pupils open the page they made in L1. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–10) Starter — what does “accessibility” mean? (think–pair–share). Pupils tick/choose on the starter worksheet (a multi-select “what helps accessibility”). • Support: choose the meaning. • Core: tick all that help. • Challenge: explain why alt text helps a screen-reader user. 2. (10–20) Optional teacher-played accessibility video (external link in the source, up to 4:00 — has sound, teacher’s choice given low-arousal default). 3. (20–30) I-do — add an image: ``. Live code; name the common errors (`scr`, missing quotes, self-closing tag). 4. (30–40) We-do — make a hyperlink ``; show an image-as-link. 5. (40–50) You-do — add one image and one working link to your page. Worksheet: matching (src/alt/width), card-sort (tag vs attribute), order (steps to add an image), challenge code (write a link). • Support: block-by-block strip. • Core: do both unaided. • Challenge: make the image itself a link. Likely error: missing quotes around the address → “the address always goes inside quote marks.” Keep a screenshot. 6. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; TA notes who added sensible alt text. Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine, not reward; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: image frames (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Mini project
lessons.objectives:
I can identify features of good and bad web design
I can link pages stored in the same folder
I can insert an image stored locally in my folder
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: web design · local · remote · folder. TA: pupils open their site from L1–L2. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — match the tags (recall of L1–L2). Pupils drag answers on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–15) We-do — the parts of a web page (anatomy image): heading, menu, content, footer. Contrast a good page with a cluttered bad page (bad-website image). Sort good vs bad on the worksheet (card-sort).
3. (15–25) Plan — a quick wireframe sketch (wireframe image); naming and purpose of two new pages.
4. (25–45) I-do/you-do — local vs remote files (folder image). A local link/image uses just a file name. Pupils make page2.html, add a local image, and link the pages with a list (order block for the steps; challenge code for a local image + nav list).
   • Support: copy the menu from page 1.  • Core: build unaided.  • Challenge: add a “home” button on every page. Likely error: link points to a file not in the folder → “check the file name and folder match exactly.” Keep a screenshot.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; pupils click their links to prove they work. TA notes who has working links between all pages.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; calm discussion of bad design using stills only.

lessons.title: Introduction to CSS
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what CSS is and why we use it as well as HTML
I can write a CSS rule with a selector, property and value
I can link my HTML page to an external stylesheet
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: CSS · style sheet · selector · property · value. TA: pupils open their HTML files. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — predict what a block of CSS would do (do NOT reveal yet; they test it). Pupils write a prediction on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–15) I-do — what is CSS? Sets the look; one stylesheet styles every page at once (one-style-many-pages image).
3. (15–25) We-do — the shape of a rule: `h1 { color: red; }` — selector, property, value. Live demo the external `<link rel="stylesheet">`; name the file `styles.css`.
4. (25–45) You-do — make styles.css, link it, change a background colour and a font. Worksheet: matching (property→effect), card-sort (inline vs external), order (steps to link a stylesheet), challenge code (a CSS rule).
   • Support: copy the example rule, change one value.  • Core: add two rules.  • Challenge: style several tags. Likely error: missing the semicolon or `}` → “every rule ends with ; and closes with }.” Keep a screenshot.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; TA notes who linked an external file rather than styling inline.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

resources.title: one style, many pages (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: DIVs and classes
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what a div is used for
I can give a div a class
I can use a class in CSS to style a section
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: div · class · style · nesting. TA: pupils open their HTML + CSS files. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — explore the code (div-code image): what is the relationship between the HTML and the CSS? Pupils answer on the starter worksheet.
2. (10–20) I-do — a `<div>` groups part of a page into a section; a class lets CSS find it. We-do: add `class="favourite"`, style `.favourite { }` (divs-code-output image).
3. (20–30) Nesting — a div inside a div (kept light, one level).
4. (30–45) You-do — wrap part of the page in a `<div class>` and style the class. Worksheet: matching (div/class/nesting), Parsons (order a div block), challenge code (CSS for a class).
   • Support: copy a class, change one value.  • Core: add a styled section.  • Challenge: nest a div. Likely error: missing the full stop in the CSS rule → “a class rule starts with a full stop, like `.header`.” Keep a screenshot.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; TA notes who used a class in BOTH the HTML and the CSS.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Layouts and the CSS box model
lessons.objectives:
I can plan a layout with a wireframe and a house style
I can describe the parts of the CSS box model
I can use margin and padding to space out elements
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: box model · padding · border · margin · float. TA: pupils have their site and CSS open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — same site, different screens. What changes on a small phone? (think–pair–share; link to L2 accessibility.)
2. (10–18) I-do — plan first: sitemap + wireframe (sitemap/wireframe images); house style = consistent colours and fonts (colour-swatches image).
3. (18–30) We-do — the CSS box model (box-model image): content → padding → border → margin. Worksheet: matching (layer→meaning), order (layers inside-out), Core text (padding vs margin; what `px` means).
4. (30–45) You-do — add padding and margin to a div, then float two boxes side by side. Challenge code (10px padding + a border).
   • Support: change one padding value and watch it move.  • Core: pad and border a div.  • Challenge: float two columns. Likely error: mixing up padding and margin → “padding pads INSIDE, margin pushes OUTSIDE.” Keep a screenshot.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; TA notes who can name where padding and margin sit. Homework: wireframe the assessed site.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Final project
lessons.objectives:
I can build a three-page website using HTML and CSS
I can use a rubric to evaluate a website
I can give and act on peer feedback
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: HTML · CSS · div · class · box model. TA: pupils ready to build their site. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — write the HTML and CSS (recall): write a list inside a `div`, then CSS to make it red and monospace. Swap and check.
   • Support: choose the right tag/class syntax.  • Core: write the CSS rule.  • Challenge: add a monospace font.
2. (15–35) I-do/you-do — the project: build a three-page website on a chosen theme, reusing earlier code. Hand out the rubric now.
3. (35–45) Self-check against the rubric — pupils slide a 1–3 rating for HTML/CSS, pages, images, accessibility, navigation (slider questions). This focuses their next steps.
4. (45–55) Plenary — peer feedback: swap with a neighbour, give two things that work and one to improve (text fields). Model kind, specific feedback.
Differentiation: the SAME build task with a low floor (one styled page) and high ceiling (three linked, well-styled pages); no flashing/sound; the site need not be finished today.

lessons.title: Project completion
lessons.objectives:
I can act on feedback to finish my website
I can show my work to others
I can answer end-of-unit questions about HTML and CSS
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: HTML · CSS · div · class · box model · float. TA: assessment criteria on display all lesson. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — review your feedback; turn it into a short to-do list (starter worksheet). Help anyone whose feedback is vague pick one concrete first job.
2. (5–25) Finish the website — work the to-do list and save where the teacher can collect it. Circulate, focus on flagged learners.
3. (25–35) Showcase — share the site with the class or just a neighbour (low-pressure). Activity worksheet: confidence sliders + show-your-work.
4. (35–50) End-of-unit quiz — 20 single-choice questions on the quiz worksheet; quiet individual work (~15 min). Read aloud for anyone who needs it. Answer key is in the conversion notes.
5. (50–55) Plenary — hand in website + quiz; congratulate the class on finishing the unit.
Differentiation: open finishing task (low floor/high ceiling); quiz read aloud on request; no flashing/sound.
